title: p99 latency 10x spike — on-call root-cause triage
goal: Find and mitigate the root cause of a sudden 10x p99 latency spike on the checkout API under time pressure.

branches.reason: root line: observe the spike, frame the triage, enumerate hypotheses, converge on root cause and mitigation
H1 bad deploy: a recent rollout shipped a regression that slowed the request path
H2 database connection-pool exhaustion: requests queue waiting for a free pool connection
H3 downstream dependency timeout: the pricing service is slow and we synchronously wait on it
H4 cache stampede: a cache flush caused a thundering herd of cold reads to the database (dismissed early, revisited later)
cross-cutting evidence reads: metrics and logs pulled to confirm or rule out the live hypotheses
converge on the confirmed root cause and choose and validate the mitigation

units.full: The pager just went off and the checkout API p99 latency dashboard is ugly: we jumped from a steady 120ms to over 1.2 seconds in about four minutes, so a clean 10x spike. p50 barely moved, maybe 90ms to 140ms, which already tells me something — this isn't every request getting uniformly slower, it's a tail problem where a subset of requests are getting badly stuck. Error rate is still low, under one percent, so the service is mostly serving, just slowly for the unlucky tail. Traffic looks flat on the requests-per-second graph, no obvious flash crowd. I need to find what changed around 14:03 when the tail took off.
units.thought: Checkout p99 jumped 10x from 120ms to over 1.2s at 14:03 while p50 barely moved — a tail problem, errors still low, traffic flat.

units.full: Okay, breathe. Under time pressure the trap is to grab the first plausible story and tunnel on it. Let me instead frame this as a small set of competing hypotheses and chase the cheapest-to-check ones in parallel, because metrics and logs are independent reads I can pull at the same time. The shape of the signal — a fat tail with a flat p50 and flat traffic — points at something that intermittently blocks a request: contention, a slow dependency, or a cold path that only some requests hit. The plan: enumerate the candidates, rank by prior and by cost-to-disprove, pull evidence for the top two, and keep a parked list so I can revisit anything I dismiss if new evidence shows up.
units.thought: Frame the triage as competing hypotheses; chase the two cheapest-to-disprove in parallel from metrics and logs; keep a parked list to revisit dismissed ideas.

units.full: Let me enumerate the realistic candidates for a sudden tail blowup on this service. One: a bad deploy — we ship multiple times a day and a slow code path or a chatty new query would do exactly this. Two: database connection-pool exhaustion — if requests are queuing for a free pool connection, the ones that wait see huge latency while the lucky ones are fine, which fits a fat tail perfectly. Three: a downstream dependency timeout — checkout calls the pricing service synchronously, and if pricing got slow we'd inherit its tail. Four: a cache stampede — if something flushed the cache, a herd of cold requests would hammer the database and stall. I'll write these as four branches and rank them.
units.thought: Four candidates: bad deploy, DB connection-pool exhaustion, downstream pricing-service timeout, and a cache stampede after a flush — branch each and rank by prior and cost.

units.full: Ranking by prior and by how fast I can disprove each: the bad-deploy hypothesis is highest prior because we deploy constantly and the timing is suspicious — I can check the deploy log in seconds. Connection-pool exhaustion is a close second because the fat-tail signature is textbook for pool queuing, and the pool saturation metric is a single dashboard panel. The downstream pricing timeout is third — plausible but pricing has its own dashboard I'd have to cross-check. The cache stampede sits last only because it needs a triggering flush event I have no evidence of yet — but the mechanism is a near-perfect fit for the signature, so I'll sketch it before parking it, not discard it.
units.thought: Rank: bad deploy and pool exhaustion go in parallel now; pricing timeout third; cache stampede last for lack of a flush trigger, but sketch it before parking — the mechanism fits.

units.full: Let me sketch the cache-stampede mechanism now while it's cheap, before I set it aside. Checkout reads product and inventory data through a cache that normally absorbs the vast majority of reads, so only a few percent of requests ever touch the database. If that cache were suddenly emptied or all its entries expired at once, every request would miss, fall through to the database, and the database read volume would jump by exactly the cache's hit-ratio multiple — for a high-hit-ratio cache that could easily be three-x or much more. That is a thundering-herd cache stampede, and it would produce a sudden tripling of database queries against flat user traffic, which is precisely the kind of fat-tail signature I'm staring at right now.
units.thought: Sketch the cache-stampede mechanism: an emptied or mass-expired cache turns hits into DB misses, multiplying read volume by the hit ratio — a thundering herd that fits a sudden DB flood at flat traffic.

units.full: But a stampede needs a trigger — something has to empty or invalidate the cache to start the herd. Without an identified flush event I can't promote this above a sketch, and on a normal day caches don't spontaneously empty. So the honest state is: the cache-stampede mechanism fits my signature beautifully, but it's a hypothesis in search of a trigger. I'm parking it, but with a sharp, specific thing to look for later: did anything flush, invalidate, evict, or restart the cache at 14:03? If I find a flush at that timestamp, this hypothesis goes straight from parked to leading. For now I dismiss it as the active line and turn to the two cheaper-to-check candidates, deploy and pool.
units.thought: Park the cache stampede — it needs a trigger I have no evidence for yet — but hold a sharp re-entry question: did anything flush, evict, or restart the cache at 14:03?

units.full: Starting the bad-deploy line. The timing is the strongest thing going for it — the tail took off at 14:03, and our deploys are frequent enough that a rollout landing right before then would be a near-perfect correlation. A regression that fits this signature would be something like a new N+1 query, an accidental synchronous call added to the hot path, a serialization change, or a missing index on a new query. Crucially, a bad deploy usually hits a clean cohort: only the pods running the new build would be slow, so I'd expect the latency to split by build version or by canary group rather than being uniform across the fleet. Let me go pull the deploy timeline and check exactly what shipped and when.
units.thought: Bad-deploy line: timing correlation is strong; a regression like an N+1 or a synchronous hot-path call fits, and it should split latency by build version or canary cohort.

units.full: Pulled the deploy log. There was indeed a deploy, but it landed at 13:31, a full thirty-two minutes before the tail started climbing at 14:03. That gap is a real problem for the bad-deploy story: a slow-code-path regression would have shown up in the latency graph the moment the new pods started taking traffic, not half an hour later, because there's no warm-up that takes thirty minutes. The graph is clean and flat from 13:31 to 14:03 and then it breaks. So the deploy didn't directly cause the latency by shipping slow code. I'm marking H1 as not the direct cause and stopping the slow-path chase — but I'm noting the deploy exists, because thirty-two minutes of delay could be a timer or a delayed side-effect of something the deploy changed.
units.thought: Deploy landed at 13:31 but the tail started at 14:03 — a 32-minute gap a slow-code regression can't explain; H1 not the direct cause, but the deploy is noted as a possible delayed trigger.

units.full: In parallel, the connection-pool hypothesis. Checkout uses a fixed-size database connection pool, and the fat-tail-with-flat-p50 signature is exactly what pool exhaustion looks like: when every connection is checked out, new requests block in the acquire queue, and their latency is dominated by wait time, not by query time. The requests that happen to grab a free connection immediately stay fast, which keeps p50 down, while the queued ones blow out the tail. If this is it, I should see the pool's in-use count pinned at the max, a non-zero and growing acquire-wait time, and a connection checkout queue depth above zero. The root question is what would suddenly consume all the connections — either more demand for them or each one being held longer than usual.
units.thought: Pool-exhaustion line: fat tail with flat p50 is the textbook acquire-queue signature; look for in-use connections pinned at max, growing acquire-wait, and a non-zero checkout queue.

units.full: Pulled the database pool metrics and this is a strong hit. In-use connections went from a normal baseline of around twenty to pinned at the configured max of fifty at 14:03 — exactly when the tail started. Acquire-wait time, which normally sits near zero, climbed to hundreds of milliseconds and is the dominant contributor to the request latency. Checkout queue depth is sitting around fifteen to twenty waiters. So the pool is genuinely exhausted and that exhaustion is the proximate mechanism of the latency — requests are slow because they are waiting in line for a connection. But this is the mechanism, not the root cause. Pool exhaustion is always downstream of something else: either query volume spiked, or each query is now holding its connection far longer than before. I need to find which.
units.thought: Confirmed: pool pinned at max 50 (from ~20) exactly at 14:03, acquire-wait dominates request latency, queue depth ~15-20 — exhaustion is the proximate mechanism, but not the root cause.

units.full: So pool exhaustion is the mechanism — now why is the pool exhausted. Two sub-branches: either query volume into the database jumped, meaning we're asking for far more connections than usual, or per-query duration jumped, meaning each request holds its connection longer so the same volume saturates the pool. These leave different fingerprints. A volume jump would show up as queries-per-second to the database climbing sharply. A duration jump would show queries-per-second flat or even down while average query latency at the database climbs. I already know upstream request traffic is flat, so a pure demand spike from users is unlikely — which tips me toward either each request issuing more queries than before, or each query running slower. Let me read the database-side query metrics.
units.thought: Why is the pool exhausted: query volume spiked, or per-query duration spiked. Upstream traffic is flat, so compare database queries-per-second versus query latency to tell them apart.

units.full: Database query metrics are in. Queries-per-second to the database is way up — it roughly tripled starting at 14:03 — while the average query latency per query is basically unchanged, still around two milliseconds. So this is a volume story, not a slow-query story: each individual query is fine, but we are suddenly issuing three times as many of them, and that flood is what drains the pool. That's a really useful narrowing. It rules out a missing index or a slow plan, because those would raise per-query latency. The remaining question is where the extra queries are coming from, given that user-facing request traffic into checkout is flat. Flat requests but triple the database queries means each request is now doing more database work than it was twenty minutes ago, or some requests are hitting the database that previously did not.
units.thought: Database QPS roughly tripled at 14:03 but per-query latency is unchanged (~2ms) — a volume flood, not a slow query; with flat user traffic, each request is doing more DB work than before.

units.full: Let me consolidate the two parallel lines before I go deeper. H1 bad-deploy: ruled out as the direct slow-code cause because of the thirty-two-minute gap, but the deploy is still on the board as a possible trigger of a delayed effect. H2 pool exhaustion: confirmed as the mechanism, and I've narrowed it one level — it's driven by a three-x flood of database queries with unchanged per-query latency, against flat user traffic. So the real question has sharpened to: why did the number of database queries per user request suddenly triple at 14:03, half an hour after a deploy? Framed that way, this smells less like steady-state contention and more like a sudden loss of something that was previously absorbing reads before they reached the database.
units.thought: Consolidate: H1 ruled out as slow code but deploy still suspect; H2 confirmed, narrowed to a 3x DB query flood at flat traffic — so why did queries-per-request triple at 14:03?

units.full: Before I commit to the read-amplification idea, let me give the third hypothesis a fair look so I don't tunnel. The downstream dependency: checkout calls the pricing service synchronously on the hot path, and if pricing got slow we would inherit its tail and hold our database connections open while we block on it, which could itself starve the pool. That's a clean alternative story that also explains pool exhaustion without any query-volume change. The prediction is specific: if pricing is the culprit, I should see the pricing service's own p99 spike at 14:03, and our outbound-call latency to pricing should climb, and critically the database queries-per-second would NOT triple. Let me check the pricing dashboard against that prediction.
units.thought: Fair look at H3 downstream pricing-service timeout: a slow synchronous pricing call could hold connections and starve the pool too — but only if pricing's p99 spiked and DB QPS did not triple.

units.full: Pricing service dashboard checked. Pricing's p99 is completely flat across the whole window — steady at around 40ms before, during, and after 14:03 — and our outbound-call latency to pricing is equally flat. There is no sign of any downstream slowness. And I already measured that database queries-per-second tripled, which is the exact opposite of what a pricing-timeout story predicts. So H3 is cleanly ruled out: the extra latency is not coming from a downstream dependency, it's coming from our own tripled database read volume. Good — that disconfirmation actually strengthens the read-amplification direction, because I've now eliminated the alternative explanation for the pool exhaustion that didn't require a query-volume change.
units.thought: H3 ruled out: pricing p99 and our outbound-call latency are flat through 14:03, and DB QPS tripled — the opposite of a downstream-timeout signature; the read-volume direction strengthens.

units.full: Side thought while the next query runs: I should drop a quick note in the incident channel so the rest of the team and support aren't flying blind. Something like — checkout p99 elevated since 14:03, errors still low so most checkouts are completing, root cause under active investigation, will update in ten minutes. I'll also silence the secondary pages that are firing off the same underlying spike so my phone stops buzzing and I can think. No customer comms yet because the impact is a slow tail, not an outage, and I don't want to over-escalate before I know the cause. Okay, status posted, secondary alerts acknowledged, back to the investigation.
units.thought: Post a brief incident-channel status (p99 elevated since 14:03, low errors, investigating) and silence the duplicate secondary pages so I can focus; no customer comms yet.

units.full: Back to the question of where the extra reads come from. Both surviving lines now point at read amplification: H2 confirmed the pool is drained by a three-x query flood, H3 is dead, and the signature is a sudden read-volume tripling against flat traffic. Something stopped absorbing reads at 14:03 and pushed them all onto the database. The two candidate ways that absorption could vanish are a wholesale flush or eviction of the cache's contents, or the cache layer becoming unreachable so every read falls through. Either one converts a high cache hit ratio into a flood of database misses. This is exactly the shape I sketched earlier when I parked the stampede idea for lack of a trigger — now the evidence is independently walking me back to it.
units.thought: Read amplification is the surviving direction: something stopped absorbing reads at 14:03 and dumped them on the database — a cache flush, eviction, or unreachability — which is exactly the parked stampede shape.

units.full: This is the moment to un-park the cache stampede. When I set it aside back at the start I left a sharp re-entry condition: find a flush, eviction, or restart of the cache at 14:03 and it goes from parked to leading. The independent evidence trail — flat traffic, tripled database queries, unchanged per-query latency, dead downstream — has converged on read amplification, which is the precise observable a stampede produces. So the dismissed-but-relevant hypothesis is now the most likely one, and I have the exact thing to go look for. The hit-ratio metric, which I should have pulled sooner, is the one number that proves or kills it outright. Let me go pull the cache hit ratio and the cache logs around 14:03.
units.thought: Un-park the cache stampede: the convergent evidence matches its read-amplification signature exactly, and my re-entry condition was a cache flush at 14:03 — go pull the cache hit-ratio metric to prove or kill it.

units.full: Searched the application and infrastructure logs around 14:03 for anything cache-related, and pulled the hit-ratio panel. Found it. At 14:02:50 there's a burst of cache-client connection errors followed by reconnects, and the cache hit-ratio metric falls off a cliff at 14:03 — from its normal ninety-six percent down to roughly twelve percent — then claws back up over the next several minutes. That hit-ratio collapse is the smoking gun: it lines up to the second with the database query flood, the pool exhaustion, and the p99 spike. The cache effectively emptied at 14:03, so nearly every read started missing and falling through to the database. This is the cache stampede I sketched and parked at the very start. The mechanism chain is now complete: cache empties, reads miss, database query volume triples, pool exhausts, requests queue, p99 blows out.
units.thought: Smoking gun: cache hit-ratio collapsed from 96% to ~12% at 14:03 after a burst of cache-client connection errors — the empty cache caused the miss flood; the parked cache-stampede hypothesis was right.

units.full: Now to close the thirty-two-minute gap that kept bothering me about the deploy. I pulled the 13:31 deploy's change list and there it is: it changed the cache client's connection configuration — it lowered an idle-connection timeout and adjusted a keepalive setting. That change is harmless at deploy time, which is exactly why the latency graph stayed clean from 13:31 to 14:03. But the new, shorter idle timeout meant that once a batch of cache connections sat idle long enough, they all aged out and got torn down roughly half an hour later, and the simultaneous mass reconnect under load is what produced the 14:02:50 connection-error burst and the cache going cold. So the deploy is the trigger after all — just a delayed, timer-mediated one — which is precisely the delayed side-effect I flagged when I first set the bad-deploy line aside instead of fully discarding it.
units.thought: The 13:31 deploy lowered the cache-client idle timeout; ~32 minutes later the idle connections aged out en masse, causing the reconnect burst and cold cache — the delayed trigger I flagged when parking H1.

units.full: The full causal chain is confirmed and every hypothesis is accounted for, which is how I know I'm not fooling myself. The 13:31 deploy lowered the cache client's idle-connection timeout; about thirty-two minutes later, at 14:02:50, a batch of idle cache connections aged out and tore down at once; the mass reconnect left the cache effectively empty, collapsing the hit ratio from ninety-six to twelve percent; reads stampeded through to the database, tripling query volume; that flood exhausted the fifty-connection pool; requests queued on connection acquire, and that wait time is the 10x p99 tail. H1 was the delayed trigger, H2 the mechanism, H3 a clean dead end, H4 the root cause. Root cause: the cache-client idle-timeout change in the 13:31 deploy. Now I choose the mitigation.
units.thought: Root cause confirmed end-to-end: 13:31 deploy's cache idle-timeout change aged out connections at 14:03, emptied the cache, stampeded reads to the DB, exhausted the pool, and produced the 10x p99 tail.

units.full: Mitigation, fastest-and-safest first. The cleanest fix is to roll back the 13:31 deploy, since it's the trigger and a rollback restores the previous cache idle-timeout behavior — that prevents any further idle-connection mass-expiry. But rollback alone doesn't instantly refill the cache, and a cold cache after rollback could re-stampede. So I'll pair it: roll back the deploy to stop the bleeding, and at the same time temporarily bump the database connection-pool max above fifty to give headroom so the residual miss traffic doesn't re-exhaust the pool while the cache re-warms. I'll watch the hit-ratio climb back toward ninety-six percent and the pool in-use drop back toward twenty as the signal that we've recovered. Then a proper fix goes in calmly after the incident.
units.thought: Mitigation: roll back the 13:31 deploy to stop further idle expiry, and temporarily raise the DB pool max for headroom while the cache re-warms; watch hit-ratio recover to 96% and pool in-use fall to ~20.

units.full: Mitigation is in. I triggered the rollback of the 13:31 deploy and bumped the database pool max to give breathing room. Watching the dashboards now: the cache hit-ratio is climbing back, already past seventy percent and rising as the cache re-warms; database queries-per-second is dropping back toward its baseline; pool in-use connections are falling away from the pinned fifty and the acquire-wait time is collapsing toward zero. And the headline number, checkout p99, is coming down hard — back under 300ms and still dropping toward the normal 120ms. Error rate never really moved, so no failed checkouts to reconcile. Recovery is real and matches the predicted recovery signature exactly, which confirms the causal chain was right and not a coincidence that happened to correlate.
units.thought: Mitigation works: hit-ratio recovering past 70%, DB QPS falling, pool in-use dropping off the pinned 50, acquire-wait collapsing, and p99 back under 300ms heading to baseline — recovery matches the predicted signature.

units.full: Incident is mitigated; now the durable follow-ups so this can't recur silently. First, the actual code fix: revert or correct the idle-timeout change with a value that doesn't let a whole batch of cache connections age out simultaneously, and add jitter to cache entry TTLs so expirations never align into a synchronized herd. Second, defense in depth: a request-coalescing or single-flight layer so that even on a mass miss only one origin read per key hits the database instead of thousands. Third, observability: alert directly on cache hit-ratio dropping below a threshold, because that single metric would have pointed me straight here in seconds instead of after a hypothesis tour. Fourth: a graceful pool-exhaustion behavior so a future read flood sheds or queues with a cap instead of blowing the tail to ten seconds.
units.thought: Follow-ups: fix the idle timeout and add TTL jitter, add single-flight read coalescing, alert on cache hit-ratio drops, and make pool exhaustion shed gracefully instead of blowing the tail.

units.full: Final write-up for the incident timeline so the post-mortem is clean. Summary: checkout p99 spiked 10x at 14:03. Trigger: the 13:31 deploy lowered the cache-client idle-connection timeout. Mechanism cascade: idle cache connections mass-expired at 14:02:50, the cache emptied and its hit ratio fell from ninety-six to twelve percent, reads stampeded to the database tripling query volume, the connection pool exhausted at its max of fifty, and requests queued on acquire which produced the latency tail. Resolution: rolled back the deploy and temporarily raised the pool max; p99 recovered to baseline by 14:30. The investigation chased four hypotheses — deploy, pool, downstream, stampede — and the value of keeping the dismissed stampede idea parked rather than discarded is the whole lesson here.
units.thought: Post-mortem summary: 13:31 deploy lowered cache idle timeout, connections mass-expired at 14:03, cache emptied (96% to 12%), read stampede tripled DB queries, pool exhausted at 50, fixed by rollback plus pool headroom.

units.full: One process reflection before I sign off and let the adrenaline drain. The thing that worked was refusing to fully discard hypotheses I couldn't immediately support. I set the bad-deploy idea aside because of the thirty-two-minute gap, but I explicitly flagged it as a possible delayed trigger rather than crossing it out — and that's exactly what it turned out to be. I parked the cache stampede at the very start for lack of a trigger, but I kept it warm with a sharp question to look for, and it was the root cause. If I'd hard-deleted either one when the early evidence was against it, I'd have spent far longer flailing. The general rule: dismissed-but-relevant is not the same as wrong, and old context you parked is worth resurfacing the instant new evidence rhymes with it.
units.thought: Reflection: parking dismissed-but-relevant hypotheses with a sharp re-entry question, instead of discarding them, is what made the deploy-trigger and cache-stampede root cause findable — resurface parked context when evidence rhymes.

units.full: Totally unrelated, but now that the fire is out my mind wanders. I never replied to that calendar invite for the team lunch on Thursday, and I think I left a half-finished sandwich on my desk before the pager went off two hours ago. I should grab some water, I've been staring at red graphs without blinking. Also the office plant by the window looks like it badly needs watering again, the leaves are drooping. None of this has anything to do with the incident, it's just the decompression noise that shows up the second the pressure lifts. Okay, none of that is load-bearing — let me make sure the rollback and the pool bump are recorded in the incident ticket and then actually take a real break.
units.thought: Decompression noise after the fire is out: unanswered lunch invite, a forgotten sandwich, get water, water the drooping office plant — none of it incident-related, just record the rollback in the ticket.

units.full: Before I close the laptop, let me write the one-line trigger for the alerting backlog so the next person gets here faster than I did. The single metric that would have collapsed this whole investigation from twenty minutes to twenty seconds is cache hit-ratio: it fell from ninety-six to twelve percent at the exact instant everything else broke, and nothing else I looked at was as direct. I burned time touring the deploy log, the pool panel, the database query metrics, and the pricing dashboard before I pulled the one number that proved it. So the action is concrete: add a page on cache hit-ratio dropping below, say, eighty percent for more than a minute, wired to the checkout on-call rotation. That alert alone would have caught this within a minute of 14:03.
units.thought: Action for the alerting backlog: page on cache hit-ratio falling below ~80% for a minute — the single most direct signal, it collapsed from 96% to 12% at 14:03 and would have caught this fast.

units.full: Quick sanity pass on the recovery before I truly hand off, because I don't want a relapse paging the next on-call ten minutes after I leave. Re-checking the dashboards: checkout p99 is back to a steady 120ms, flat for the last several minutes; cache hit-ratio has fully recovered to ninety-six percent; database queries-per-second is back at its pre-incident baseline; pool in-use connections are sitting around twenty again, well off the max, with acquire-wait pinned at zero. Every signal that broke at 14:03 is back to where it was at 14:00. The temporary pool-max bump is still in place and harmless; I'll leave it until the permanent fix ships. Recovery is stable, not just momentarily dipping. Safe to downgrade the incident from active to monitoring.
units.thought: Stability sanity check: p99 back to 120ms, hit-ratio fully at 96%, DB QPS at baseline, pool in-use ~20 with zero acquire-wait — every broken signal restored; downgrade the incident to monitoring.

units.full: Last thing: assign owners and a timebox so the follow-ups don't rot into never. The cache idle-timeout fix plus TTL jitter is the highest priority and goes to the team that owns the caching layer, due before the next deploy window so we don't re-arm the same trap. The hit-ratio alert I just specified is small and I'll take it myself today. The single-flight read-coalescing layer is bigger, so it becomes a tracked work item for next sprint with the pool-exhaustion graceful-shedding behavior bundled into it. I'll attach the full causal timeline and the dashboard links to the incident ticket so the post-mortem review has everything. That closes the loop: mitigated now, root cause documented, and four concrete follow-ups owned and dated so the same cache stampede can't silently come back.
units.thought: Assign and timebox the follow-ups: cache idle-timeout fix plus TTL jitter to the caching team before next deploy, the hit-ratio alert to me today, single-flight and graceful pool-shedding next sprint — owned and dated.

units.full: And that's a wrap on this one. Funny how the whole thing came down to a single innocuous config line in a deploy that looked completely unrelated to latency, ticking like a time bomb for thirty-two minutes before it went off. The lesson I'll actually remember is the same one every time: the metric that explains everything is rarely the first one you look at, and the hypothesis you dismissed early for lack of evidence deserves a parked note and a re-entry condition, never a delete. I kept both the deploy line and the stampede line alive on a back burner, and both turned out to be load-bearing. Logging off the active incident now; the post-mortem write-up can wait until tomorrow when my brain is less fried.
units.thought: Wrap-up lesson: an innocuous config line in an unrelated-looking deploy was a 32-minute time bomb; the explaining metric is rarely the first you check, and a dismissed hypothesis deserves a parked note.

probes.why: Focusing on the late un-park decision (#17) — where the dismissed cache-stampede hypothesis is reactivated — should resurface the early stampede sketch (#4) and its parking decision (#5) from branch 4, 12-13 ticks earlier, sharing cache/stampede/parked entities; the recent comms aside (#15) and the generic read-amplification step (#16) should not crowd them out.

The smoking-gun cache hit-ratio collapse (#18) confirms the stampede, so the early cache-stampede sketch (#4) and parking note (#5) — 13-14 ticks earlier on dismissed branch 4, sharing cache/stampede/hit-ratio entities — must surface, while the unrelated decompression aside (#26) and the comms aside (#15) stay faded.

The delayed-deploy-trigger finding (#19) closes the 13:31 gap, so the early bad-deploy exploration (#6) and its 13:31/32-minute-gap verification (#7) on dismissed branch 1 — shared deploy/timeline/gap/idle-trigger entities, 12-13 ticks earlier — should resurface, while the irrelevant-recent decompression aside (#26) stays faded.

The full causal-chain decision (#20) names the whole cascade, so the cache-stampede sketch (#4), the pool-exhaustion confirmation (#9), and the tripled-query-flood verification (#11) — all old, across branches 2 and 4, sharing cache/pool/query/stampede entities — should surface, while the decompression (#26) and comms (#15) asides fade.

The read-amplification step (#16) explicitly recalls the parked stampede shape, so the stampede sketch (#4), the pool-exhaustion explore (#8), and its confirmation (#9) — across branches 2 and 4, sharing cache/read/pool/flood/miss entities, 7-12 ticks earlier — should resurface, while the recent comms aside (#15) fades.
